content.problemStatement:
A public wall at your university is littered with posters advertising various events.  A new policy has just been enacted that states no two posters on the wall may overlap.  You have been asked to remove some posters from the wall so the remaining posters  do not obscure each other.  To keep the students as happy as possible, you should remove the minimum number of posters to achieve this goal.
You may not remove a poster and place it in another position; all posters you leave on the wall must be in their original position.



When you examined the wall, you noticed something very nice.  Every poster was placed on the wall by pinning the four corners.  This was done in a very courteous manner since each pin goes only through the poster it is holding.



Time to get to work! Oh, the reason you were hired is that you are very good at taking down a single poster without disturbing the rest, even if that poster is obscured by many others.
constraints: x0 < x1 and y0 < y1 for every poster
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 25), the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 100), the number of posters. The next n lines each contain four integers x0 y0 x1 y1 (x0 < x1, y0 < y1) describing the lower‑left and upper‑right corners of a poster.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the maximum number of posters that can remain on the wall such that no two posters share any common point.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, we can keep the first and third posters. For the second test case, we can only keep one poster.

solution.approach: Two posters overlap if their projections intersect in both x and y directions, i.e. !(x1_i <= x0_j || x1_j <= x0_i || y1_i <= y0_j || y1_j <= y0_i). Build an undirected graph G where vertices are posters and an edge connects two overlapping posters. The required answer is the size of a maximum independent set in G. For n ≤ 100 we can solve this exactly by finding a maximum clique in the complement graph (edges connect non‑overlapping posters) using a branch‑and‑bound Bron–Kerbosch algorithm with bitset representation. The algorithm recursively expands a candidate clique, pruning whenever the current clique size plus the number of remaining candidates cannot exceed the best solution found so far. Bitsets (two 64‑bit words) represent vertex sets, giving fast set operations. The answer for a test case is the size of the maximum clique found, which equals the maximum number of pairwise non‑overlapping posters.
solution.timeComplexity: Worst‑case exponential, roughly O(2^{n/2}) for n = 100, which is acceptable for the given limits; typical instances run in milliseconds.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n^2/64) for the adjacency bitsets plus O(n) recursion depth (≤ 100).
